full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I come from a long, rich line of Bible readers.<|5.88|> <|5.88|> My grandmothers, maternal and paternal, were voracious students of the scriptures.<|13.32|> <|13.32|> My maternal grandmother, in particular, taught me and my family how to write notes in the margins of our Bibles.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To this day, if a sermon is particularly moving, or I have a question, or I just want to remember something, I will highlight or write in my Bible a practice that helps the ancient texts feel especially contemporary and resonant.<|19.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When I was studying the readings for this 13th Sunday after Pentecost, I wrote in the margins of Deuteronomy,<|9.50|> <|10.50|> can it really be this simple?<|13.12|> <|14.30|> Can it really be as simple as a choice between life and death, blessing and curse?<|23.44|> <|23.44|> Amen.<|24.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Can life be split so easily into these two categories of choices?<|6.64|> <|7.82|> In the same way that we don't come to church to be saved, I hate to break it to you,<|13.28|> <|13.84|> but to point us toward the things and people and forces that save us,<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We don't come to church for simple answers to complex questions.<|6.54|> <|7.38|> At least I don't. I can only speak for myself.<|10.76|> <|12.04|> We come to church trusting that God is wise and patient enough to sit in the tension of our questions with us.<|23.52|> <|23.52|> Amen.<|24.30|>
